summary: Gruber argues that Electron apps represent a serious threat to native Mac software quality, but the deeper problem is the declining demand for well-made native apps. He traces this to the Mac's surge in popularity a decade ago, which brought users who don't recognize or care about platform conventions. He draws a parallel to Microsoft Word 6.0, which Mac users rejected in the 1990s for being un-Mac-like, noting that today's users happily tolerate far worse offenders like Google Docs in Chrome. Most damningly, he points out that even Apple itself is shipping Mac apps that violate basic platform conventions, from the Marzipan apps to a new App Store that doesn't support Page Down. He concludes that the biggest threat to the Mac isn't competing platforms but apathy toward what makes great Mac apps great.
key_points: Electron is a symptom, not the disease — the real problem is declining user demand for native Mac app quality
The Mac's popularity surge brought users who don't know or care about platform conventions, diluting the community that once rejected bad apps
In 1996, Mac users rejected Microsoft Word 6.0 for being un-Mac-like; today they happily use Google Docs in Chrome, which is far worse
Apple itself is contributing to the decline by shipping Marzipan apps and an App Store app that fails basic Mac conventions like Page Up/Down support
The biggest threat to the Mac isn't iPads, Chromebooks, or Windows — it's apathy toward what makes great Mac apps great
money_quotes: Electron is without question a scourge.
I think the Mac will prove more resilient than Windows, because the Mac is the platform that attracts people who care. But I worry.
The worst thing that ever happened to the Mac is that it got so much more popular a decade ago.
What the Mac market flatly rejected as un-Mac-like in 1996 was better than what the Mac market tolerates, seemingly happily, today.
Software no longer needs to be Mac-like to succeed on the Mac today. That's a tragedy.
The problem with Electron apps isn't really Electron — it's the decline in demand for well-made native Mac apps.
The biggest threat to the Mac isn't iPads, Chromebooks, or Windows 2-in-1's — it's apathy towards what makes great Mac apps great.
Developers at Apple, of all companies, should know that design is how it works.
key_insight: The real threat Electron poses to the Mac isn't technical — it's that users no longer demand the native quality that once defined the platform, and even Apple has stopped holding the line.
